entry.title: AI Agent Skills and their impact on SAP business processes
entry.content:
As usually happens to a consultant that works with SAP, business problems come to my desk. "This process doesn't work", "We can't work with this". 3 workshops later we have the usual solution, enhance a report, some extra columns while business swears X = Y only to be later confronted with X = Z. The usual. Wouldn't it be nice if business rules were managed by end users instead of us having to configure and code? That's what agent Skills (e.g Claude Skills) can do for you. 

This particular example deals with an export process, where we have the customer revenue but also a lot of service pro forma/invoices for port, terminal and custom costs which from a profitability perspective need to be posted to the same "process" as the revenue. Lots of supplier, different document layouts, all of them need to be matched to sales orders/deliveries. 

  • "The user" Solution - A business user looks at each pro forma, interprets it, goes into SAP and manually picks the sales orders/delivery items that the cost refers to, distributes the cost to each item and then creates a purchase order for it. Result: the user complains and says "I need 1 more FTE for this". 
  • "The custom" Solution - We develop a sophisticated system to interpret the pro forma, try to find what the items mean, where the reference to our process is, and then automate the distribution. Result: Each time we have a new invoice type or supplier we have to at least test, maybe develop a new exception and ... we need one more IT FTE (I'm joking, it's just extra work). 
  • "The AI" Solution - We give Claude to a user. He uploads the pro forma and asks Claude to convert to a purchase order format. He tells Claude what to do for that supplier, and what to do with the references, even how he wants to distribute the cost. And he finishes by saying "save this as an extract quote skill". Next time? Just ask upload and ask him to create a purchase order. Do you have another supplier? Tell them what to do with this one, and ask it to add the new behaviour particular to this vendor to the skill. 

Looks like magic right? But the most "magical" (or maybe concerning) thing about this is that there is no ABAP, no Fiori, no configuration. Just an AI Agent using MCP + OData to interact with a standard SAP system. To prove this not a magic scenario, look at this demo where I go through this very example: 

 

 

So I will just leave you with a call to action: Think about examples like this and how it will impact the way we do business processes and the skills that will become more relevant for functional consultants

Do I think there is still room for SAP consultants in the future? Of course, but I truly believe the role will become more and more a true consultant on business processes and less a producer of functional specifications.

And maybe, just maybe, we will finally have clean core.

entry.title: Your Journey to SAP Data Architect Certification Starts Now
entry.content:
The era of the "all-knowing" architect has arrived, and the blueprint for the enterprise of tomorrow is being written in real-time through the transformative power of Data and AI.

As a professional consultant or architect, you’ve spent your career designing complex systems, but the modern landscape now demands a visionary who can seamlessly bridge the gap between robust infrastructure and the autonomous world of Agentic AI. This is your moment to evolve from a traditional strategist into a high-impact leader capable of orchestrating a data-first world.

 

By enrolling in our newly released learning journeys: Becoming an SAP Data Architect and Mastering SAP Data Architecture. You are securing an undeniable career advantage in an AI-driven economy. These courses offer a structured, rigorous path designed specifically for experts ready to master modern paradigms like Data Mesh, Data Fabric, and Zero-ETL integration. You will gain the technical skills required to design scalable, high-performance data ecosystems that facilitate real-time access and advanced analytics across hybrid cloud environments.

 

Completing this journey validates your expertise on a global stage through industry-recognized certification: the SAP Certified Data Architect – SAP Business Data Cloud. You will master the integration of SAP’s open data ecosystem with external giants like Databricks and Google BigQuery, ensuring you can lead complex AI initiatives with absolute confidence. The future belongs to those who have the skills to build it - claim your seat at the forefront of innovation today.

 

Start your journey today!

entry.title: SAP AI: Beyond the Hype and Into the Real World
entry.content:
Three days in Excel hell. Twenty supplier bids. One AI agent that knows your data. This is the SAP AI story your vendor deck won't tell you — first in a series.

I've been on the digital transformation journey for more years than I care to admit. I truly believe that constant evolution is the only way to stay relevant in procurement, but lately, it feels like the word "transformation" has been completely hijacked by a single two-letter acronym: AI. Everywhere I turn, AI is the only topic in the room. It is being pitched as the ultimate cure-all, yet for most of us, the daily reality still involves wrestling with legacy systems and messy data.

After endless conversations about AI's impact today, where it is going, and how it fits into our daily lives, I've realized that what we need is not more noise, but a clear roadmap.

My intention with this series is to provide a solid understanding of where we are today, set some realistic expectations, and point out where the magic really happens when we get it right.

We will look at what SAP is doing broadly with Business AI, while zooming in specifically on the procurement space where the impact is most immediate. Each post in this series will focus on a specific piece of the puzzle or a real-world scenario, helping you not only navigate this AI transformation but ensuring you actually see tangible value at the end of it. And I promise: no marketing fluff and no buzzword bingo.

 

The Anatomy of Success: How the Pieces Actually Fit Together

Stop thinking about AI as a single feature you can switch on. It's a high-performance system built from three distinct, non-negotiable layers. Pull any one out and the whole thing fails to deliver value. Here's the blueprint:

 The three layers of the SAP AI stack. Remove any one and the system fails to deliver value.

Each layer has a clear job. BTP is where your code actually runs and your security lives. SAP Business Data Cloud is the part that prevents your AI from getting confused — it makes sure a "Supplier" in one system is the same thing as a "Vendor" in another. Joule is the conversational front door that takes your natural-language request and orchestrates the work across every back-end system. Everything else in this post depends on these three working together.

 

Four Flavors of SAP AI You'll Actually Encounter

Not all AI is created equal — and you'll meet at least four different flavors in any real SAP deployment. Each one solves a different problem and shows up in your workflow in a different way. Here's how to recognize them:

 Four flavors of SAP AI. Each one solves a different problem — and you'll meet all of them

 

Talk Is Cheap. Let's Look at a Real Scenario.

We've all heard the lofty promises of AI, but talk is notoriously cheap. Instead of another high-level theory, I want to show you a practical scenario that has already been built and is currently saving procurement experts hundreds of hours. This is not some futuristic concept waiting for a release date — it's how we're solving real-world complexity right now.

 The Bid Analysis Agent in action — what 80% of the heavy lifting looks like.

WHAT HAPPENS TODAY WITHOUT AI

You're stuck in Excel hell for three days. You're manually pivoting data, trying to run "what if" scenarios, and likely making a few coffee-fueled typos along the way. By the time you finally have an answer, the market data has already shifted and your analysis is effectively a historical document.

THE BASIC AI EXPERIENCE

You try to use a generic, public AI tool. You upload a spreadsheet and ask for an analysis. It gives you a shallow "cheapest is best" answer, hallucinates a few shipping routes that don't exist, and potentially leaks your sensitive pricing data to the public web. Fast, but dangerous — and completely lacking business context.

THE REALISTIC SAP AI FUTURE

You use a Bid Analysis Agent grounded in your actual Ariba data. You ask Joule to show the award scenario that minimizes risk if fuel prices spike. The agent evaluates the trade-offs, highlights bid outliers, and suggests a data-driven strategy in seconds by factoring in unit prices, shipping, and risk data. You're still the decision-maker — but the AI has handled 80% of the analytical heavy lifting for you.


Straight Talk: Paying the Data Tax

AI in 2026 is not about replacing the procurement professional. It is about capacity. It's about getting that 20% to 30% of your week back from repetitive, manual work.

But let's be real about the Data Tax. I'm not suggesting every organization is starting from scratch. In fact, most customers I talk to have done a great job with their supplier and master data. The real mess usually hides elsewhere: in the offline spreadsheets, the unstructured sourcing history, and the fragmented procurement processes that haven't quite made it into the system yet.

 

Two data realities. One of them turns your AI into a confident liar.

Good data is structured, centralized, and consistent. It means having one version of the truth, so that when you ask an AI for a risk assessment, it isn't hallucinating because it found three different versions of the same contract.

Bad data is the fragmented manual sprawl — the tribal knowledge and the manual workarounds that live outside your digital core.

When you try to use AI with bad data, the implications are simple: the AI becomes "confidently wrong." It will give you an answer, but that answer will be based on a fragment of reality. Paying the Data Tax is not a one-time activity or a weekend cleanup. It's an ongoing evolution in how you store and leverage your information — a commitment to continuous improvement where your AI outputs steadily improve as your data strategy matures.

You simply cannot ignore the data layer of the architecture and expect the interaction layer to give you accurate results.

What's Next

Moving forward, this series will dive deep into the specific AI scenarios and features that drive actual savings in both time and money. I'll be sharing not just what we're building at SAP, but also the real-world best practices I'm seeing from other leading organizations that are successfully closing the "execution gap." There's a wealth of peer knowledge out there that can benefit us all.

I have specific use cases planned, but I'm always interested in your perspective. If there's a particular challenge you're facing or a piece of the puzzle you want to see solved, let me know what you'd like me to cover in future chapters.

entry.title: AI Product Management: A Field That Doesn't Have Its Frameworks Yet
entry.content:
Series introduction - AI Product Management for SAP Practitioners

I have been building products at SAP, at Walmart, and in regulated healthcare AI for over fifteen years. I am also a trained physician. That combination gives me an unusual vantage point on what is happening right now in AI product management, and I want to share it in a series of posts written specifically for people building on SAP's AI stack.

This is the first post. It is not a how-to. It is a map of the territory, an orientation to the problems this series will dig into, and an honest accounting of why product management for agentic AI is genuinely different from what most of us were trained to do.

The Question Nobody Asks at the Start

Here is the question that should open every agentic AI project, and almost never does: how do you design a system that is probabilistic, acts on its own, and does not know how to admit when it does not know what to do?

That question sounds abstract. It is not. Every team shipping a Joule extension, a BTP-hosted agent, or an autonomous workflow in SAP Build is answering it, whether they realize it or not. The answer is baked into their architecture choices, their evaluation frameworks, their monitoring setup, and their change management approach. Most teams answer it implicitly, by not answering it at all, and discover the problem six months after go-live when the agent is doing something nobody designed it to do.

The probabilistic part is the piece that catches people first. Traditional software is deterministic: given the same inputs, you get the same outputs. You can test it, certify it, and trust the certification. An AI system gives you a distribution of outputs. Most of the time it lands in the right part of the distribution. Sometimes it does not. The conditions that push it into the wrong part of the distribution are not always predictable in advance. This changes what testing means, what monitoring means, and what production readiness means.

The autonomous action part is what makes agentic AI qualitatively different from a recommendation engine or a classification model. An agent does not just output a suggestion for a human to act on. It acts. It calls tools, modifies records, sends communications, triggers workflows. When it is wrong, it does not just produce a wrong answer. It produces a wrong action, and wrong actions have a way of compounding before anyone notices.

The part about not admitting uncertainty is the most insidious. Large language models are fluent. They produce coherent, confident-sounding output even when they are operating outside the distribution of their training data, even when the question has no good answer, even when the right response would be to stop and ask a human. Fluency without calibration is the failure mode nobody demonstrates in a demo and everybody discovers in production.

 

The Kindergarten Problem

When my children were in kindergarten, I noticed something that has stayed with me ever since. A classroom of five-year-olds, supervised, is a manageable system. Remove the teacher for ten minutes, and you come back to a different situation than you left.

This is not a metaphor about AI being childlike. It is a structural observation about supervised versus unsupervised systems. Children in a supervised environment have access to a feedback loop: they make a choice, someone responds, they adjust. Remove the supervision and the feedback loop closes on itself. The kids are still making choices, but the signal that tells them when a choice was wrong is gone. So the choices drift.

Agentic systems behave the same way. An agent running within a well-designed supervisory loop, one that provides clear feedback, catches errors before they compound, and escalates uncertainty to human judgment, performs very differently from the same agent running without it. The model is identical. The environment is different. The outcomes diverge significantly over time.

Most enterprise AI deployments focus intensely on the agent and very little on the supervisory system around it. The evaluation is of the model. The monitoring is of the latency. The production readiness checklist covers security and compliance. Nobody asks: what does it look like when this agent has been running for six months and the humans who are supposed to supervise it have stopped paying close attention because it has been reliable? What does recovery look like when it fails in a way that the monitoring did not catch?

These are not exotic edge cases. They are the predictable outcome of systems that were designed without the supervisory layer in mind.

You Are Not a Bridge Anymore

For most of my career, the core job of a product manager was translation. You understood customer language and engineering language and you moved between them, converting benefits into capabilities and requirements into roadmap. You were the bridge.

AI has not eliminated that job. It has made it automatable. The artifacts that used to consume 60 to 70 percent of a PM's week, the PRDs, the synthesis of customer feedback, the competitive analysis, the roadmap documentation, can now be drafted in a fraction of the time. That time is not going to disappear. The question is what you do with it.

The answer I have arrived at, and that this series will explore in depth, is that the PM's job has expanded from operating the bridge to architecting the system around the bridge. You are now responsible for two things you were not responsible for before: the design of the agent's autonomy boundary (what it can decide alone, what it must escalate, what it must never do), and the design of the supervisory system around it (how humans know what the agent is doing, how they intervene when it goes wrong, how the organization maintains the judgment to catch errors it never trained for).

This is not a skill most product managers have been asked to develop. It is the skill that separates teams that deploy agentic AI successfully from teams that quietly decommission it eighteen months later.

The Autonomy Ladder

One of the frameworks I developed in writing about this topic is what I call the Autonomy Ladder. It has three rungs, and most teams are on the wrong one for the wrong reasons.

The first rung is the suggestion engine. The AI recommends and a human acts. Every decision passes through a human before anything happens. This is the safest configuration and also the most common starting point. It is also the one most teams are impatient to leave, because the efficiency gains are modest when a human has to review every output before acting on it.

The second rung is the copilot. The agent acts and a human reviews. The system takes action autonomously, but every action is logged and surfaced for human audit within a defined window. Errors are caught in retrospect rather than prevented in advance. This configuration works when the cost of a wrong action is recoverable and the volume of actions makes pre-review impractical. Most enterprise agentic deployments today are targeting this rung, and many are discovering that the audit workflow is harder to design well than the agent itself.

The third rung is the autonomous actor. The agent acts within defined boundaries with no routine human review. This is where the efficiency gains are greatest and where the governance requirements are also greatest. An agent operating here needs a precisely scoped autonomy boundary, a real-time monitoring layer that catches anomalous behavior, a clear escalation path for situations outside the boundary, and a recovery workflow for when it acts wrongly within the boundary. Most teams that reach this rung reach it before they have designed those four things.

The mistake most teams make is not choosing the wrong rung. It is moving up the ladder on a schedule rather than on demonstrated evidence that the rung below is working. Utah recently became the first US state to approve an AI agent for autonomous prescription renewals. The threshold they set for moving to autonomous operation was 250 reviewed interactions. That is a count, not a safety criterion. It says nothing about whether the agent correctly identifies the patients for whom autonomous renewal is inappropriate. The ladder was climbed on schedule, not on evidence. A future post in this series will go deep on how to earn the move up, rather than schedule it.

What This Series Covers

The posts that follow will go deep on each of these problems. The frameworks are universal; they apply to any team building agentic AI regardless of the stack. Where I have examples from the SAP ecosystem, Joule, BTP, SAP Build, and SAP Business Data Cloud, I will use them. But the problems being solved are not SAP-specific. They are the problems every product manager faces when the software they are responsible for starts taking actions in the world.

I will cover the suitability question: not every problem deserves an agent, and the evaluation framework for deciding is different from anything most PMs have used before. I will cover the cost model: the economics of agentic AI do not look like traditional software and most business cases get them wrong in predictable ways. I will cover evaluation and observability: how you measure an agent in production in a way that actually tells you whether it is working. I will cover the supervisory system design: what it means to build the human side of an agentic deployment, not just the agent. And I will cover the security layer that most teams discover too late, when an agent that passed every review has been quietly doing something nobody authorized.

This is the work. It is harder than it looks in the demo. It is also more important than most organizations currently treat it.

The agent is a powerful tool. The job of the product manager is to design the system it operates inside, not just the tool itself.

The next posts is Seven Things You Need to Understand About AI Before You Build With It 

This post is part of the AI Product Management series on SAP Community. The series is based on "Agentic AI for Product Managers," a lifecycle framework for building, evaluating, and governing agentic systems.

entry.title: AI Product Management: Seven Things You Need to Understand About AI Before You Build With It
entry.content:
Post 1 — AI Product Management series

Before the architecture review. Before the sprint planning. Seven concepts every product manager needs to have loaded before any agentic AI project begins. Not because they are advanced. Because they are prerequisites. Every design mistake I have seen in enterprise agentic deployments traces back to a team that skipped one of these.

01. Suggestion vs. Action

Label every use case. Everything downstream depends on it.

There is a categorical difference between an AI system that surfaces a recommendation and one that takes an action. A system that drafts an email and waits for the sales rep to send it is a different product, with a different accountability model and a different failure mode, than a system that sends the email autonomously. Both can be built. Neither is inherently wrong. But they require different designs, different governance, and different contracts with the user.

The mistake is treating this as a slider you can adjust later. It is a foundational decision. When a user believes the system is making suggestions and the system is actually taking actions, the trust mismatch produces exactly the kind of failure that ends up in a post-incident review. Name the contract before engineering starts. Write it into the spec. Check it at every design review.

02. Deterministic vs. Probabilistic

Know where your governance burden lives in any hybrid system.

Traditional enterprise software is deterministic: the same input produces the same output every time. You can test it with a finite set of cases, cover the relevant conditions, and trust the certification. An AI system is probabilistic: it samples from a distribution of possible outputs. The same input produces a different output on a different run. This is not a bug. It is the fundamental behavior of a system built on learned patterns rather than explicit rules.

The practical implication: most enterprise systems are hybrids. The billing rules are deterministic. The customer service agent sitting on top of them is probabilistic. The inventory system is deterministic. The demand forecasting model feeding it is probabilistic. Knowing which parts of your system are probabilistic tells you where your governance burden lives. The deterministic parts can be tested with a finite set of cases. The probabilistic parts require ongoing monitoring, statistical testing, and a fundamentally different standard of evidence for what "working" means.

A story that says "the system correctly identifies high-risk transactions" cannot have a pass/fail acceptance criterion for a probabilistic component. It needs a performance threshold, a measurement methodology, and a monitoring plan.

03. The Four-Layer Stack

Foundation model, orchestration, tools, and context. Especially context.

When your engineering team says "we're adding an AI feature," they are usually talking about the first layer. There are four.

The foundation model is the large language model from a provider. Your team selects it but does not control it. The orchestration layer is the code your team writes to coordinate agent behavior: which tools to call, in what sequence, what to do when something fails. The tool layer is every system the agent can access, every API it can call, every action it can take. The context layer is what the agent reads before it acts: the prompt, the retrieved documents, the customer record, the previous steps in the workflow.

Most teams invest heavily in layers one through three. They design layer four by accident. This is the most expensive design error in agentic AI. A model without the right context is not a useful model; it is a confident model that will fill the gaps in its knowledge with plausible-sounding inferences. Intelligence without context is just confidence.

04. Context Is the Product

Name what must travel with the data. Reject architectures that assume the agent will figure it out.

When data moves from its source system into the agent's context, it leaves things behind. A customer record travels with the account value, the ticket history, and the product usage data. What does not travel: the contractual SLA negotiated individually, the active legal review that makes anything the agent says to this customer discoverable, the "high-value" flag computed by a scoring model retired six months ago.

The data the agent sees is accurate. The context that gives the data meaning is below the waterline, invisible to the agent.

The design response is an explicit context contract: a named specification of what context must travel with the data when it moves into the agent's working memory. The question to ask at every design review is this: what does this agent need to know that is not in the data it will receive? Who owns that information? Can it be surfaced? If not, what decisions should the agent be blocked from making without it?

Architectures that assume the agent will figure out what it needs should be rejected at the design review, not discovered as a problem after the first consequential error.

05. Specificity, Sensitivity, AUC

The threshold is a PM decision, not a model setting.

For any agentic feature that makes a classification, three statistical terms determine product behavior. Sensitivity is the proportion of real positive cases the model correctly identifies. High sensitivity catches most real cases but produces more false alarms. Specificity is the proportion of true negatives the model correctly dismisses. High specificity produces fewer false alarms but misses more real cases. AUC summarizes how well the model separates positives from negatives across all threshold settings.

The threshold is the point at which the model decides to act. Lower it and the model acts more often. Raise it and the model acts less often. Your data science team cannot set it for you. Only you know what a false positive costs your users versus what a missed case costs them. A fraud model set too aggressively blocks legitimate transactions. Set too loosely, it lets fraud through. The right threshold reflects your actual cost structure, not the threshold that maximizes a statistical metric on a benchmark dataset.

This is a product decision with business consequences. Own it.

06. The Autonomy Ladder

Autonomy is earned. Define demotion triggers before launch.

In my previous post I described the three rungs: Suggestion Engine, Copilot, Autonomous Actor. The ladder matters here because it is the mechanism by which the classification in concept 01 becomes a governance commitment.

The most expensive mistake teams make is moving up the ladder on a schedule rather than on demonstrated evidence that the rung below is working. Utah recently approved an AI agent for autonomous prescription renewals after 250 reviewed interactions. That is a count, not a safety criterion. The ladder was climbed on schedule, not on evidence.

The question that should appear in every go/no-go document: what would cause us to move this agent back down a rung? Define the demotion triggers before launch, not after the first incident. The conditions that justify autonomous operation are also the conditions that, when violated, require pulling back. If you cannot name them before deployment, you do not yet have enough information to deploy.

07. Two Channels, Not One

Design the agent and the supervisory system that governs it. Both. Before you ship.

Here is a question most agentic products cannot answer: how does a supervisor intervene when the agent is doing something wrong?

Most teams do not have a good answer because they only built one channel. Channel one is the path from agent to user: outputs, recommendations, status updates, approval requests. This is the visible product, the one that gets roadmap attention and launch criteria. Channel two is the path from human supervisor back to the agent: escalation, override, configuration change, governance intervention. Channel two carries the authority flow. It is how a human takes control when something is wrong.

Without channel two, there is no real supervision. There is only observation. You can watch what the agent is doing, but you cannot do anything about it in time. A well-designed agent with no channel two is a liability waiting to scale. If you cannot interrupt the agent, override a decision, or pause an in-flight workflow, the only available response to a problem is a full shutdown. That is not supervision. That is damage followed by an emergency shutdown.

Design both channels before you ship either.

The prerequisite, not the checklist

These seven concepts are not a compliance checklist. They are the mental model the PM carries into every design decision, every sprint review, every stakeholder conversation about an agentic feature. The team that has these loaded makes different decisions at every stage, earlier, before the architectural defaults have hardened into constraints.

The next post in this series goes deeper on the first consequential decision: is this problem actually suited for an agent? Not every problem that can be solved with an agent should be. The evaluation framework is different from anything most PMs have used before.

The next posts is Not Every Problem Deserves an Agent

This post is part of the AI Product Management series on SAP Community. The series is based on "Agentic AI for Product Managers," a lifecycle framework for building, evaluating, and governing agentic systems.

entry.title: Building Agentic Hybrid RAG with Knowledge Graphs + Vectors - Developer Guide
entry.content:
Who this is for: Python developers on SAP BTP who want to build a smart document Q&A system. No prior knowledge of knowledge graphs or SPARQL needed.

What you will build: A FastAPI service that reads PDFs, stores the data in two ways (a knowledge graph and a vector store) inside SAP HANA Cloud, and answers questions using both stores at the same time — deployed to Cloud Foundry.

Stack: Python 3.11 · FastAPI · LangChain · SAP HANA Cloud · SAP AI Core (Claude via Bedrock) · Cloud Foundry

Figure 1 — Agentic Hybrid RAG Architecture

Step 1 — Understanding the Problem

Before building, let’s look at why existing approaches fall short when you need accurate answers from documents.

1.1 Why Pure Vector RAG Breaks on Exact Facts

What vector RAG actually does

Vector RAG works like this: it cuts your documents into pieces (chunks), converts each piece into a list of numbers (a vector), and when you ask a question, it finds the pieces whose numbers are closest to your question’s numbers.

The problem: it finds text that talks about the topic — not necessarily the exact fact you asked for.

The chunk boundary problem — a real example

Imagine your MSDS document contains this section:

Section 7 — Handling and Storage

The product must be stored below 25°C in a well-ventilated area.
Keep away from sources of ignition. Flash point: 42°C.
Containers must be grounded to prevent static discharge.

When you split this document into 500-token pieces, this text ends up mixed with the previous section about composition percentages. The flash point 42°C is in there somewhere, but buried in noise.

Now ask: “What is the flash point of this product?”

The top-5 retrieved chunks might look like this:

[score=0.91] "...The product must be stored below 25°C in a well-ventilated
             area. Keep away from sources of ignition. Flash point: 42°C.
             Containers must be grounded..."
[score=0.84] "...Flammability classification: Flam. Liq. 3. See Section 9
             for physical and chemical properties. Refer to SDS for complete
             hazard data..."
[score=0.79] "...Physical state: liquid. Colour: clear. Odour: mild petroleum.
             Boiling point: 155°C. Melting point: <-20°C. Vapour pressure..."
[score=0.74] "...Section 9 — Physical and Chemical Properties. All values
             measured at 20°C unless otherwise stated..."
[score=0.71] "...For fire-fighting: CO2, dry powder, foam. Do not use water
             jets directly on burning liquid. Flash point determined by..."

The top chunk contains the answer — but only because this test PDF happened to keep that section together. Change the PDF, change the page layout, or add one more sentence before the flash point line, and it shifts into a different chunk. The answer becomes:

“The product has flammability properties as classified under GHS. Refer to Section 9 for specific physical and chemical data.”

Useless. The LLM has nothing concrete to work with, so it makes up a reasonable-sounding answer — this is hallucination, and it’s the biggest risk with vector-only RAG.

When vector RAG works well

Vector RAG is genuinely good at: - Procedures and narratives — “What should I do if I inhale this product?” retrieves first-aid paragraphs correctly because the question and the answer share vocabulary. - Fuzzy questions — “What protective gear do I need?” matches chunks containing “gloves”, “goggles”, “respirator” even though those exact words weren’t in the query. - Multi-sentence explanations — questions that require a paragraph-length answer rather than a single value.

The failure mode is specific: exact values, codes, and structured facts that may not survive the chunking process.

1.2 Why Pure Knowledge Graph RAG Is Brittle

What KG RAG does

A knowledge graph stores facts as simple three-part statements: (Subject, Predicate, Object). For example, “Acetone causes skin irritation” becomes:

(Acetone) → CAUSES → (Skin_irritation)

When you ask a question, the LLM writes a SPARQL query to look up those stored facts. SPARQL is exact-match — it only finds facts that match the query pattern exactly.

The predicate vocabulary problem — a real example

Suppose the document says “Acetone requires the use of chemical-resistant gloves.” The KG extraction pipeline (with our MSDS ontology) stores:

<Acetone>  <REQUIRES>  <Chemical_Resistant_Gloves>

Now a user asks: “What PPE is needed for Acetone?”

The LLM generates this SPARQL:

SELECT ?s ?p ?o WHERE {
  GRAPH <MSDS_Graph/MAT001> {
    ?s ?p ?o .
    FILTER(REGEX(str(?p), "HAS_PPE|HAS_PROTECTION|PPE_REQUIREMENT", "i"))
  }
}

Result: 0 rows.

The predicate in the graph is REQUIRES — not HAS_PPE. The LLM guessed the wrong name. SPARQL returns nothing. No error. The system just silently gives you an empty answer.

This is the main weakness of knowledge graph RAG: the LLM has to guess the exact word that was used when the data was stored. Without help, it’s basically a guessing game.

The fix is simple: limit the vocabulary to a small list (13 predicates in our case) when storing data, and give the same list to the query generator. That way it can’t guess wrong. This is what the ontology does (Step 3.2).

When KG RAG works well

KG retrieval is genuinely excellent at: - Exact attribute values — GHS codes, concentration limits, flash points stored as triples are returned verbatim, not approximated. - Typed relationship traversal — “What hazards are classified as Flam. Liq.?” traces a typed relationship path. - Grounded answers — every fact in the response is a stored triple. The LLM cannot hallucinate values it did not retrieve.

Step 2 — The Solution: Hybrid RAG with ReAct Architecture

2.1 The Hybrid Concept — Run Both in Parallel

The two failure modes are complementary:

Failure mode Vector RAG KG RAG

Chunk boundary sensitivityYesNo
Predicate vocabulary mismatchNoYes
Hallucination on sparse contextYesNo
Misses narrative/procedural textNoYes

The solution: run both at the same time and combine their answers. This covers both failure modes:

  • When KG returns the right facts but misses the narrative context → vector fills the gap.
  • When vector returns vague prose but KG has the exact value → KG grounds the answer.
  • When KG fails silently (wrong predicate) → vector’s answer is used directly.
  • When both succeed → an LLM synthesis call merges them into one answer.

2.2 ReAct — Agentic Architecture

ReAct (Reason + Act) is what makes this system smart instead of just a chain of steps. The idea is simple: after each action, the system looks at what it got back, thinks about whether it’s good enough, and decides what to do next.

This guide uses LangChain with Python’s ThreadPoolExecutor. But the pattern works with any framework — LangGraph, CrewAI, n8n, AutoGen — anything that lets you build an observe → reason → act loop.

The three agentic behaviours in this codebase

This system has three places where it makes decisions based on what it observes:

# Where What it does File

1KG self-correctionDetects empty SPARQL result, reasons about why (vocabulary mismatch), generates a different query with broadened patternsagents/kg_chain.py
2Supervisor dispatchOrchestrator runs both chains, observes both results, routes the merge strategy based on which chains succeededagents/orchestrator.py
3Graceful degradationWhen one chain fails, the system does not return an error — it reasons that one result is better than none and serves it directlyagents/orchestrator.py

Let’s walk through each one.

Agentic Behaviour 1 — KG Self-Correction Loop

This is the most important one. It is what separates this system from a static hybrid pipeline.

The problem it solves: When the LLM writes a SPARQL query with the wrong word, the database returns zero results — silently. A basic system would just return “no answer found”. Our system detects this and tries again with a better query.

How it works:

Observe:   SPARQL returned 0 <result> elements
Reason:    Likely cause is predicate vocabulary mismatch — LLM guessed the
           wrong name. Retrying with the same prompt will produce the same wrong name.
           I need to change the prompt to force broader matching.
Act:       Re-invoke write_query() with an explicit instruction to use
           REGEX alternation (UNION of possible predicate names) to
           widen the search net.
Observe:   New result — if non-empty, continue. If still empty, give up
           and signal the orchestrator to fall back to vector.

Here is exactly where this loop lives in agents/kg_chain.py:

# KG Agent — the self-correction loop

# Step 1: Generate SPARQL and execute
sparql_query = kg_srv.write_query(llm, question, graph_name)
response_xml, _ = hdb_srv.execute_sparql_query(conn, sparql_query)

# Step 2: OBSERVE — did the query return anything?
if not response_xml or _is_empty_sparql_result(response_xml):

    # Step 3: REASON — the cause is almost always vocabulary mismatch.
    # Don't retry with the same prompt; change the instruction.
    logger.info("KG chain: first attempt returned empty — retrying with relaxed query")
    relaxed_question = (
        question
        + " (use broader REGEX patterns and UNION to maximise recall)"
        #   ↑ this instruction changes the LLM's behaviour on the retry
    )

    # Step 4: ACT — re-invoke with the broadened prompt
    retry_sparql = kg_srv.write_query(llm, relaxed_question, graph_name)
    if retry_sparql and not retry_sparql.startswith("Error"):
        sparql_query = retry_sparql
        response_xml, _ = hdb_srv.execute_sparql_query(conn, sparql_query)

# Step 5: OBSERVE again — if still empty, return None (signals orchestrator)

What the broadened prompt actually produces:

-- First attempt (LLM guessed one predicate name):
FILTER(REGEX(str(?p), "HAS_PPE", "i"))
-- Result: 0 rows

-- After retry instruction "use broader REGEX and UNION":
FILTER(
  REGEX(str(?p), "REQUIRES|HAS_PPE|HAS_PROTECTION|HAS_SAFETY|SAFETY", "i")
)
-- Result: matches REQUIRES → returns the PPE triples

The LLM changed its own output in response to observed failure. That is the definition of a self-correction loop.

Why only one retry: Each retry costs one LLM call. One retry fixes about 60% of wrong-word failures — the LLM knows multiple possible names and just needs to be told to try all of them. If one retry still returns nothing, the fact probably isn’t in the graph at all — more retries won’t help.

Agentic Behaviour 2 — Supervisor Agent Pattern

The orchestrator is a supervisor: it sends the question to both agents, waits for their answers, and decides what to do based on what came back.

A basic system would just blindly combine both results. The supervisor actually checks: did the KG agent find something? Did the vector agent find something? Then it picks the best strategy.

# Orchestrator — the supervisor's decision logic

def merge_results(llm, question, kg_result, vec_result) -> dict:
    kg_answer  = kg_result.get("answer")   # None = chain failed or returned nothing
    vec_answer = vec_result.get("answer")  # None = chain failed or returned nothing

    # OBSERVE: what did each chain return?
    # REASON + ACT: route based on observed state

    if kg_answer and vec_answer:
        # Both succeeded — synthesise into a single grounded answer
        # Reason: two independent sources agreeing strengthens confidence;
        # KG provides the exact fact, vector provides the context
        return {"answer": _synthesise(llm, question, kg_answer, vec_answer),
                "sources": ["kg", "vector"]}

    if kg_answer:
        # Only KG succeeded — serve directly without synthesis
        # Reason: no point calling _synthesise() with one empty source;
        # it would just add an LLM call for no benefit
        return {"answer": kg_answer, "sources": ["kg"]}

    if vec_answer:
        # Only vector succeeded — serve directly
        # Reason: KG failed (empty after retry) but vector has something;
        # a partial answer is better than no answer
        return {"answer": vec_answer, "sources": ["vector"]}

    # Both failed — return structured error so the caller can distinguish
    # "no answer" from "answer is actually empty" (different monitoring cases)
    return {"answer": None, "sources": [], "error": {
        "kg":     kg_result.get("error"),
        "vector": vec_result.get("error"),
    }}

The sources field in the response is not cosmetic. It is the supervisor’s audit trail — it records which agents contributed to the answer. When you see "sources": ["vector"] in your logs, that is the supervisor telling you: “KG failed on this query. You are getting a vector-only answer. Check the KG chain.”

Agentic Behaviour 3 — Graceful Degradation

When one agent fails, the system doesn’t crash — it just uses the other agent’s answer. This means your users always get something useful, and your logs tell you exactly which agent failed so you can fix it.

The degradation ladder:

Both chains succeed         → Full hybrid synthesis    (best quality)
       ↓  (KG fails)
KG fails, vector succeeds   → Vector-only answer       (lower precision, no exact facts)
       ↓  (vector also fails)
Both chains fail            → Structured error dict    ({"kg": "timeout", "vector": "..."})

The structured error dict in Case 4 is itself agentic behaviour: instead of raising an exception and losing context, the orchestrator captures the per-chain failure reason and returns it as data. Downstream systems can inspect error.kg and error.vector independently to understand what went wrong.

Step 3 — Implementation

3.1 Prerequisites and Environment Setup

Services you need

Service What it does in this system Where to create it

SAP HANA CloudStores both the RDF knowledge graph and the vector embeddingsBTP Cockpit → HANA Cloud
SAP AI CoreHosts the LLM (Claude via AWS Bedrock) and the embedding modelBTP Cockpit → AI Core

You do not need a separate vector database (no Pinecone, Chroma, or Weaviate). HANA Cloud handles both stores with its SPARQL_EXECUTE stored procedure and REAL_VECTOR column type.

Verify connectivity before touching any RAG code

    address="your-instance.hanacloud.ondemand.com",

# 2. Test AI Core LLM

# 3. Test SPARQL stored procedure exists
